image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Leonardo's portrait is famous for its subtle expression, atmospheric landscape, and sfumato modeling. It became a central image of Renaissance portraiture and museum culture.

image.alt: Unknown English or French artist's Wilton Diptych
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: This portable royal altarpiece joins jewel-like color, gold ground, and delicate courtly detail. Its paired panels make English International Gothic style easy to recognize.

image.alt: Giotto di Bondone's Lamentation (The Mourning of Christ)
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Giotto arranges grief in a shallow, believable space, giving sacred figures weight and emotion. The fresco is a landmark in the move toward Renaissance naturalism.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Duccio's great altarpiece blends Byzantine gold-ground splendor with tender narrative scenes. It anchors Sienese painting at the dawn of the Renaissance.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Masaccio uses mathematical perspective to open an illusionistic chapel wall. The fresco's solemn figures and spatial logic made it a touchstone of early Renaissance painting.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Van Eyck turns domestic space into a field of symbols, reflections, and microscopic detail. The convex mirror and oil technique make it instantly recognizable.

image.alt: Hubert and Jan van Eyck's Ghent Altarpiece
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: The opened altarpiece unfolds a dense vision of salvation, saints, donors, and luminous landscape. Its scale and oil technique transformed Northern Renaissance art.

image.alt: Hieronymus Bosch's The Garden of Earthly Delights
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Bosch fills a triptych with Eden, earthly excess, and surreal punishments. Its bizarre creatures and crowded symbolism make it one of art history's great visual puzzles.

image.alt: Sandro Botticelli's The Birth of Venus
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Botticelli sets Venus on a shell with flowing contour, pale light, and mythic grace. The painting became an emblem of Florentine Renaissance humanism.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A grove of mythological figures moves across a dark orange orchard in crisp, rhythmic lines. The work is prized for its poetic mystery and refined courtly elegance.

image.alt: Leonardo da Vinci's The Last Supper
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Leonardo stages the apostles' reactions to betrayal with dramatic grouping and exact perspective. Its damaged surface still defines the image of the biblical meal.

image.alt: Leonardo da Vinci's Lady with an Ermine
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: The sitter turns with alert intelligence while the ermine echoes her poise. Leonardo's soft modeling and psychological immediacy make the portrait a Renaissance landmark.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Raphael gathers ancient philosophers in a grand architectural setting. Balanced composition, clear gestures, and ideal form make it a model of High Renaissance harmony.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Two nearly touching hands compress the drama of creation into one electric gap. The Sistine Chapel fresco became one of the world's most reproduced images.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Michelangelo covers the altar wall with a storm of twisting bodies around Christ. Its muscular drama pushed Renaissance ideals toward a darker, monumental intensity.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Grunewald's altarpiece shifts from harrowing Crucifixion to radiant Resurrection. Its expressive color and suffering body gave devotional painting unusual emotional force.

image.alt: Hans Holbein the Younger's The Ambassadors
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Holbein surrounds two diplomats with instruments, textiles, and a stretched skull. The portrait joins worldly knowledge with a sharp reminder of mortality.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Titian transforms the reclining nude into a richly colored, intimate interior scene. The direct gaze and warm paint handling influenced centuries of figure painting.

image.alt: Paolo Veronese's The Wedding at Cana
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Veronese turns a biblical miracle into a vast Venetian banquet of architecture, music, and color. Its theatrical scale makes it a triumph of late Renaissance spectacle.

image.alt: Pieter Bruegel the Elder's The Hunters in the Snow
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Hunters return through a frozen village landscape where daily life spreads into the distance. Bruegel's winter scene is a classic of Northern Renaissance observation.

image.alt: Pieter Bruegel the Elder's The Tower of Babel
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Bruegel imagines Babel as a colossal, unfinished spiral city. Tiny workers and crumbling masonry turn ambition, labor, and divine judgment into one memorable image.

title: The Burial of the Count of Orgaz

image.alt: El Greco's The Burial of the Count of Orgaz
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: El Greco divides earthly funeral from heavenly vision with elongated figures and flashing color. The painting fuses local legend with spiritual intensity.

image.alt: Caravaggio's The Calling of Saint Matthew
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A beam of light cuts into a tavern-like room as Christ calls Matthew. Caravaggio's everyday realism and sharp chiaroscuro define Baroque drama.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Christ is recognized in a burst of gesture, still life, and theatrical light. Caravaggio makes sacred revelation feel startlingly immediate and physical.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Gentileschi gives the biblical heroine force, focus, and bodily conviction. The compressed action and stark light make the scene one of Baroque art's fiercest images.

image.alt: Nicolas Poussin's Et in Arcadia ego
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Poussin's shepherds read a tomb inscription in an ordered classical landscape. The quiet scene turns mortality into measured Baroque reflection.

title: The Anatomy Lesson of Dr. Nicolaes Tulp

image.alt: Rembrandt van Rijn's The Anatomy Lesson of Dr. Nicolaes Tulp
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Rembrandt organizes a group portrait around a public dissection. Focused faces, diagonal lighting, and the exposed arm make civic portraiture unusually dramatic.

image.alt: Rembrandt van Rijn's The Night Watch
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Rembrandt turns a militia portrait into a burst of movement, light, and overlapping figures. The painting is a defining monument of Dutch Baroque ambition.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Velazquez paints a royal studio scene that folds viewer, painter, mirror, and court into one puzzle. Its spatial wit still feels startlingly modern.

image.alt: Johannes Vermeer's Girl with a Pearl Earring
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Vermeer's tronie uses a dark background, luminous skin, and a glinting pearl to create an intimate encounter. It is prized for restraint and optical delicacy.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A servant pours milk in a quiet room where light turns ordinary surfaces luminous. Vermeer's stillness and texture make domestic work monumental.

image.alt: Jean-Antoine Watteau's The Embarkation for Cythera
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Watteau's lovers drift between flirtation, theater, and dreamlike landscape. The painting helped define the Rococo fete galante as elegant, wistful leisure.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A woman arcs through foliage on a swing while secret admirers watch. Fragonard's pink silk, garden light, and playful intrigue make it a Rococo icon.

image.alt: Jacques-Louis David's Oath of the Horatii
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: David stages Roman civic duty with hard contours, clear gestures, and austere architecture. The painting became a manifesto for Neoclassical severity.

image.alt: Jacques-Louis David's The Death of Socrates
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Socrates reaches calmly for poison while followers collapse around him. David turns philosophical martyrdom into a disciplined Neoclassical lesson in virtue.

title: The Intervention of the Sabine Women

image.alt: Jacques-Louis David's The Intervention of the Sabine Women
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: David places Hersilia between armed Romans and Sabines to halt violence. The polished bodies and moral theme show Neoclassicism after the Revolution.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Fuseli makes sleep uncanny with a collapsed body, staring horse, and crouching demon. The image channels Romantic fascination with terror and the subconscious.

image.alt: Francisco Goya's The Third of May 1808
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Goya isolates a doomed civilian in lantern light before a firing squad. The painting's blunt violence made it a model for modern images of war and resistance.

image.alt: Theodore Gericault's The Raft of the Medusa
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Gericault turns a shipwreck scandal into a pyramid of bodies, hope, and despair. Its scale and raw suffering made Romantic history painting urgent and political.

title: Wanderer above the Sea of Fog

image.alt: Caspar David Friedrich's Wanderer above the Sea of Fog
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A solitary figure faces mountains swallowed by mist, inviting the viewer into sublime uncertainty. The back-turned wanderer became a signature Romantic image.

image.alt: Eugene Delacroix's Liberty Leading the People
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Delacroix personifies liberty striding over barricades with flag raised. The painting joins revolutionary politics, allegory, and Romantic coloristic energy.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Constable paints a rural wagon crossing a quiet mill stream with fresh sky and broken light. The scene shaped the idea of English landscape painting.

image.alt: J. M. W. Turner's The Fighting Temeraire
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Turner shows an old warship towed toward dismantling under a glowing sunset. The image mourns heroic history while celebrating atmospheric paint.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Millais surrounds Shakespeare's drowning Ophelia with botanically precise plants and jewel-like water. Beauty and death meet in a haunting Pre-Raphaelite image.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Hayez turns a clandestine embrace into a charged image of passion and patriotism. The medieval setting and theatrical color made it an Italian Romantic icon.

image.alt: Emanuel Leutze's Washington Crossing the Delaware
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Leutze makes the Revolutionary crossing a heroic, windswept tableau. Its dramatic boat, flag, and icy water became a durable image of American resolve.

image.alt: Gustave Courbet's A Burial at Ornans
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Courbet gives ordinary townspeople the scale once reserved for history painting. The broad funeral scene announced Realism's refusal of idealized subjects.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Three women bend to gather leftover grain after harvest. Millet's quiet monumentality made rural labor a central subject of Realist painting.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Manet confronts viewers with a modern nude, flat lighting, and a direct gaze. The painting shocked Paris and helped redirect figure painting toward modern life.

image.alt: Edouard Manet's Le Dejeuner sur l'herbe
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A nude woman sits with clothed men in a modern picnic that refuses mythological disguise. Manet's blunt staging helped ignite modern painting.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Whistler reduces portraiture to quiet profile, gray-black harmony, and severe geometry. The image became both a formal experiment and a cultural emblem of motherhood.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Wood's stern pair and pointed farmhouse window condense rural America into an unforgettable icon. The painting balances satire, regional pride, and unease.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Hopper isolates late-night figures in a glowing diner against empty streets. The painting's clean geometry and silence made urban loneliness iconic.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Wyeth places Christina Olson in a sparse field, reaching toward a distant farmhouse. The restrained scene turns bodily limitation into psychological suspense.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Monet's loose view of Le Havre harbor gave Impressionism its name. Broken color, fog, and the orange sun make perception itself the subject.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Degas studies dancers at rehearsal rather than on stage, catching awkward pauses and disciplined bodies. The tilted room and cropped view feel candid and modern.

title: Woman with a Parasol - Madame Monet and Her Son

image.alt: Claude Monet's Woman with a Parasol - Madame Monet and Her Son
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Monet catches his wife and son in wind, light, and a low viewpoint. The brushwork makes atmosphere and fleeting outdoor experience the real subject.

title: Bal du moulin de la Galette

image.alt: Pierre-Auguste Renoir's Bal du moulin de la Galette
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Renoir fills a Paris dance garden with flickering light, social motion, and soft color. The crowded leisure scene is a classic of Impressionist modern life.

image.alt: Gustave Caillebotte's Paris Street; Rainy Day
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Caillebotte gives modern Paris a cool, spacious order of umbrellas, wet pavement, and new boulevards. Its cropped viewpoint feels almost photographic.

image.alt: Edouard Manet's A Bar at the Folies-Bergere
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Manet sets a barmaid before a mirror that complicates space, spectatorship, and commerce. The glittering surface captures modern Parisian nightlife.

image.alt: Pierre-Auguste Renoir's Luncheon of the Boating Party
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Renoir gathers friends on a riverside balcony in warm color and dappled light. The painting turns modern leisure into a lively web of glances and gestures.

image.alt: Vincent van Gogh's The Starry Night
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Van Gogh turns a night view into a turbulent pattern of stars, wind, and village lights. Its expressive brushwork made it a key image of Post-Impressionism.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Van Gogh's yellow flowers become an experiment in color, surface, and emotional intensity. The series is among his clearest statements of expressive still life.

image.alt: Vincent van Gogh's Cafe Terrace at Night
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A glowing cafe spills yellow light beneath a deep blue sky. Van Gogh's tilted space and vibrating color turn an Arles street corner into charged memory.

image.alt: Vincent van Gogh's The Potato Eaters
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Before his bright southern palette, Van Gogh painted peasants around a dim table in earthy tones. The rough hands and faces stress labor and shared poverty.

image.alt: Vincent van Gogh's Self-Portrait with Bandaged Ear
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Van Gogh presents himself after crisis with calm pose, thick color, and a bandaged head. The portrait makes identity, suffering, and paint inseparable.

title: A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte

image.alt: Georges Seurat's A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Seurat builds a park scene from disciplined dots of color and frozen silhouettes. The painting made pointillism a monumental modern language.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Cezanne reduces card players to solid volumes, muted color, and patient structure. The series helped bridge Impressionist seeing and later modern abstraction.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Rousseau sets a sleeping figure, mandolin, and lion in a moonlit desert with dreamlike clarity. Its naive precision gives the scene uncanny power.

title: Where Do We Come From? What Are We? Where Are We Going?

image.alt: Paul Gauguin's Where Do We Come From? What Are We? Where Are We Going?
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Gauguin stages a Tahitian allegory of life, death, and spiritual questioning. Broad color fields and symbolic figures show Post-Impressionism turning inward.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Klimt wraps an embracing couple in gold, pattern, and ornamental surface. The painting is the best-known image of his Viennese decorative style.

image.alt: Gustav Klimt's Portrait of Adele Bloch-Bauer I
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Klimt dissolves portraiture into gold leaf, mosaic-like pattern, and a poised sitter. Its later restitution history added to its fame and cultural weight.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Munch turns anxiety into a wavering figure, blood-red sky, and vibrating landscape. The image became a shorthand for modern psychological distress.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Matisse reduces bodies, land, and sky to ringing color and rhythmic line. The linked dancers make joy feel primal, decorative, and monumental.

image.alt: Henri Matisse's Blue Nude (Souvenir de Biskra)
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Matisse gives the reclining nude heavy contours, cool color, and sculptural force. Its anti-classical body helped push painting toward Fauvist freedom.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Kandinsky floods the canvas with collisions of line, color, and spiritual energy. The work is a landmark in the move toward nonobjective painting.

image.alt: Ernst Ludwig Kirchner's Berlin Street Scene
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Kirchner's sharp figures, acidic colors, and tilted street capture the tension of modern Berlin. The painting is a key image of German Expressionism.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Malevich presents a stark black form on white as a radical break from representation. The painting became a founding icon of Suprematism and abstraction.

title: Composition with Red Blue and Yellow

image.alt: Piet Mondrian's Composition with Red Blue and Yellow
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Mondrian balances black lines, white fields, and primary colors with severe clarity. The painting turns abstraction into a disciplined visual grammar.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Picasso fractures five figures into jagged planes and masklike faces. The painting is a disruptive precursor to Cubism and twentieth-century modernism.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Braque compresses village houses into blocky volumes and muted color. The work helped launch the Cubist focus on structure over naturalistic appearance.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Picasso made Guernica as a stark response to the bombing of the Basque town. Its fractured monochrome figures became an enduring anti-war image.

image.alt: Pablo Picasso's Girl before a Mirror
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Picasso splits the figure and reflection into bright pattern, profile, and shadow. The painting turns self-image and desire into a Cubist psychological drama.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: A woman's grief is broken into acid color, sharp planes, and clenched forms. The image extends Guernica's anguish into an unforgettable portrait.

image.alt: Salvador Dali's The Persistence of Memory
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Dali's melting watches sag across a barren dream landscape. The crisp illusionism and impossible objects made Surrealist time feel unstable and iconic.

image.alt: Rene Magritte's The Treachery of Images
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Magritte pairs a pipe with text denying that it is a pipe. The painting's deadpan contradiction became a classic lesson in image, language, and representation.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Two figures kiss through cloth-covered heads, making intimacy strange and unreachable. Magritte's calm style intensifies the Surrealist disturbance.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Kahlo doubles herself into linked figures with exposed hearts and stormy sky. The self-portrait turns identity, pain, and national dress into personal mythology.

title: Self-Portrait with Thorn Necklace and Hummingbird

image.alt: Frida Kahlo's Self-Portrait with Thorn Necklace and Hummingbird
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Kahlo faces the viewer amid thorns, animals, and lush leaves. The painting makes suffering and symbolic self-fashioning direct, controlled, and memorable.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Pollock's layered drips spread across the surface without a central figure or horizon. The painting exemplifies action painting's all-over energy.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Pollock lets skeins of black, white, and earth color pulse across a huge canvas. The all-over field records gesture as the painting's central event.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Diagonal blue bars interrupt Pollock's dense web of poured paint. The work shows Abstract Expressionism balancing improvisation with commanding structure.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: De Kooning's figure is built from slashing brushwork, grinning features, and unstable form. It keeps representation alive inside Abstract Expressionism's turbulence.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Rothko stacks hazy fields of rust, blue, and red so color seems to breathe. The painting represents the contemplative color-field side of Abstract Expressionism.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Warhol repeats supermarket soup labels with cool, mechanical clarity. The series made mass branding, seriality, and everyday consumption central to Pop Art.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Warhol repeats Marilyn Monroe's publicity image in blazing color and fading black-and-white. Celebrity, reproduction, and mortality collide in one Pop Art icon.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Lichtenstein enlarges comic-book combat into crisp outlines, Benday dots, and explosive text. The work turns mass-media style into monumental Pop Art.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Hockney freezes a California pool splash beside flat modern architecture. The clean color, stillness, and leisure imagery made it a Pop-inflected modern classic.

title: Boy and Dog in a Johnnypump

image.alt: Jean-Michel Basquiat's Boy and Dog in a Johnnypump
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Basquiat gives a child and dog raw line, electric color, and streetwise urgency. The painting shows how contemporary figuration absorbed graffiti and expressionist energy.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Wiley seats Obama amid dense botanical pattern, joining official portraiture with contemporary questions of image, history, and representation.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Cimabue's monumental altarpiece enthrones the Virgin among angels and prophets on a towering gold ground. Its Byzantine formality, softened by hints of real depth, made it a bridge to the naturalism of his pupil Giotto.

title: Annunciation with St. Margaret and St. Ansanus

image.alt: Simone Martini's Annunciation with St. Margaret and St. Ansanus
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Simone Martini's gold-ground altarpiece for Siena Cathedral catches the Virgin recoiling from Gabriel's embossed greeting. Its swaying line and courtly elegance epitomize Sienese Gothic painting.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Giotto gives the enthroned Virgin real weight and a believable space, breaking with flat Byzantine convention. Painted for Florence's Ognissanti church, it marks a turning point toward naturalism in Western painting.

title: Portrait of a Man in a Red Turban

image.alt: Jan van Eyck's Portrait of a Man in a Red Turban
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Van Eyck's man in a flamboyant red chaperon meets the viewer's gaze directly, most likely the painter himself. The frame's motto 'Als Ich Can' and minute oil detail make it a touchstone of early Netherlandish portraiture.

image.alt: Paolo Uccello's The Battle of San Romano
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Uccello turns a Florentine cavalry clash into a pageant of perspective, with broken lances laid along the orthogonals. Its rocking-horse steeds and bold geometry show the early Renaissance obsession with constructed space.

image.alt: Andrea Mantegna's Lamentation over the Dead Christ
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Mantegna foreshortens the dead Christ feet-first toward the viewer, compressing the body into a stark sculptural slab. Its radical perspective and gray pallor make it one of the most arresting images of the Renaissance.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Durer faces the viewer frontally like an icon of Christ, fur-trimmed and long-haired, asserting the artist's God-given creative power. It is the boldest of his self-portraits and a landmark of Northern Renaissance self-fashioning.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Durer's watercolor study renders a wild hare with astonishing fidelity, its fur built from thousands of layered strokes. A touchstone of Northern Renaissance naturalism and one of the most reproduced nature studies in art.

image.alt: Giovanni Bellini's Portrait of Doge Leonardo Loredan
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Bellini portrays Venice's doge in brocaded robes of state with the calm permanence of a marble bust, modeled in clear Venetian light. It set the standard for official portraiture in oil.

image.alt: Hans Holbein the Younger's Portrait of Henry VIII
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Holbein fixed the defining image of Henry VIII: broad-shouldered, frontal, and magnificently dressed. Copied across Tudor England after the original mural burned, it turned royal portraiture into an instrument of power.

image.alt: Parmigianino's Madonna with the Long Neck
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Parmigianino elongates the Virgin's neck and fingers into elegant artifice, with a single unfinished column behind. Its refined distortion of natural proportion is the textbook example of Mannerist style.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Arcimboldo portrays Emperor Rudolf II as the Roman god of the seasons, his face composed entirely of fruits, flowers, and vegetables. The witty composite head is the most famous of his painted puzzles.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: El Greco rearranges Toledo beneath a churning green-black storm sky, trading topographic truth for spiritual drama. It stands among the first great expressive landscapes in Western art.

title: Conversion on the Way to Damascus

image.alt: Caravaggio's Conversion on the Way to Damascus
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Caravaggio throws Saul flat beneath his horse, arms flung open to a blinding light only he perceives. The radical close-up and deep tenebrism made this Cerasi Chapel canvas a manifesto of Baroque religious drama.

title: David with the Head of Goliath

image.alt: Caravaggio's David with the Head of Goliath
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Caravaggio's somber David holds out Goliath's severed head, which bears the fugitive painter's own features. Made near the end of his life, it reads as both a plea for pardon and a stark meditation on guilt.

title: The Storm on the Sea of Galilee

image.alt: Rembrandt van Rijn's The Storm on the Sea of Galilee
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Rembrandt's only seascape pitches Christ and the panicking disciples on a cresting wave of light. Stolen from Boston's Gardner Museum in 1990, it remains the most famous missing painting in the world.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Rembrandt freezes the instant divine writing blazes across the wall as Belshazzar twists in terror and wine spills from golden vessels. A bravura Baroque display of light, brocade, and theatrical shock.

image.alt: Georges de La Tour's Magdalene with the Smoking Flame
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: La Tour's Magdalene meditates by a single oil lamp, a skull on her lap, in near-total darkness. His serene candlelit geometry gives Baroque tenebrism a stillness closer to silence than drama.

image.alt: Diego Velazquez's Portrait of Innocent X
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Velazquez caught the pope so truthfully that Innocent X reportedly pronounced it 'too real.' Its crimson intensity later obsessed Francis Bacon, who painted dozens of screaming variations.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Fabritius's small trompe-l'oeil panel shows a chained goldfinch on its feeder in plain daylight. Painted the year he died in the Delft gunpowder explosion, it links Rembrandt's studio to Vermeer's quiet luminosity.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Watteau's life-size sad clown stands isolated in white satin while fellow comedians mill below. Its melancholy beneath the theater costume made it an enduring emblem of the artist as outsider.

image.alt: Jean-Honore Fragonard's A Young Girl Reading
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Fragonard's lemon-yellow profile of a girl absorbed in her little book is painted with dashing, creamy speed. A beloved Rococo image of private pleasure and effortless brushwork.

image.alt: Elisabeth Vigee Le Brun's Self-Portrait in a Straw Hat
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Vigee Le Brun presents herself fresh-faced and confident, palette in hand, in open homage to Rubens. The portrait advertised the skill that made her Marie Antoinette's favorite painter.

image.alt: Benjamin West's The Death of General Wolfe
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: West painted a modern British general dying at Quebec in contemporary uniform but with the pathos of a Lamentation. It scandalized academic convention and redefined history painting around current events.

image.alt: John Singleton Copley's Watson and the Shark
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Copley dramatizes a real shark attack on a young sailor in Havana harbor as rescuers lunge from a rowboat. Its news-story subject and theatrical horror anticipated Romantic disaster painting.

title: Napoleon I on His Imperial Throne

image.alt: Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres's Napoleon I on His Imperial Throne
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Ingres enthrones Napoleon frontally with scepter and golden laurel, half Jupiter, half Carolingian icon. Its hieratic precision unsettled critics but became a defining image of imperial absolutism.

image.alt: Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres's Portrait of Monsieur Bertin
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Ingres's press magnate leans forward with hands clamped on his knees, embodying the confident bourgeoisie of the July Monarchy. Its unsparing realism within flawless drawing made it a landmark of portraiture.

image.alt: William Blake's The Ancient of Days
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Blake's crouching deity Urizen reaches down from a fiery disc with golden compasses to circumscribe creation. The visionary frontispiece to 'Europe a Prophecy' became his most iconic single image.

image.alt: Caspar David Friedrich's The Abbey in the Oakwood
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Monks bear a coffin through the ruined choir of a Gothic abbey among bare oaks in winter twilight. Friedrich's funereal landscape fuses German Romantic piety, death, and the sublime.

image.alt: Caspar David Friedrich's The Sea of Ice
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Friedrich stacks shattered ice slabs into a jagged pyramid that crushes a barely visible shipwreck. The Arctic disaster scene is Romanticism's bleakest statement of nature's indifference to human ambition.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A dog's head peers over a dark slope into a vast empty field of ochre, one of the Black Paintings Goya made on the walls of his house. Its desolate near-abstraction has haunted painters ever since.

image.alt: Eugene Delacroix's The Death of Sardanapalus
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Delacroix's Assyrian king watches impassively from his bed as his concubines, horses, and treasure are destroyed around him. Inspired by Byron, its swirling crimson violence is Romanticism at full pitch.

title: Rain, Steam and Speed - The Great Western Railway

image.alt: J. M. W. Turner's Rain, Steam and Speed - The Great Western Railway
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Turner dissolves a Great Western locomotive crossing Maidenhead bridge into driving rain and golden haze, a hare racing ahead of it. Modern speed swallowed whole into Romantic atmosphere.

image.alt: Frederic Edwin Church's The Heart of the Andes
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Church's ten-foot panorama compresses Ecuador's ecology from snowcap to jungle into one luminous view. Exhibited as a single-painting spectacle with opera glasses, it crowned Hudson River School ambition.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Millet monumentalizes a peasant striding down a darkening hillside, flinging seed with a fixed rhythm. Its heroic treatment of field labor unsettled Salon audiences and deeply influenced Van Gogh.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Daumier crowds a weary grandmother, a nursing mother, and a sleeping boy into the cheap seats of a railway car. His broad, graphic handling gives modern urban poverty a quiet monumental dignity.

image.alt: Vasily Vereshchagin's The Apotheosis of War
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A pyramid of skulls under circling crows on a ruined steppe, dedicated 'to all great conquerors, past, present and to come.' Vereshchagin distilled his battlefield reportage into one antiwar emblem.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Sargent's portrait of Virginie Gautreau in a plunging black gown, with haughty profile and lavender-pale skin, scandalized the 1884 Salon and drove him to London. He later called it the best thing he had done.

image.alt: Ivan Shishkin and Konstantin Savitsky's Morning in a Pine Forest
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Shishkin painted the mist-filled pines and dawn light; Savitsky added the four playing bear cubs. Endlessly reproduced, famously on Russian candy wrappers, it is Russia's most familiar forest landscape.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Homer strands a lone Black sailor on a dismasted sloop circled by sharks, a waterspout looming behind. Painted after his Bahamas voyages, it is his starkest image of man against the sea.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A lone woman in a cloche hat stares into her coffee cup in a late-night automat, the black window behind reflecting only two rows of receding lights. Hopper's quintessential image of urban solitude.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Rossetti paints Dante's Beatrice in ecstatic trance as a red dove delivers a poppy, an elegy for his wife Elizabeth Siddal, who died of laudanum. A defining Pre-Raphaelite image of love and loss.

image.alt: William Holman Hunt's The Light of the World
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Hunt's lantern-bearing Christ knocks at a long-shut, weed-choked door that has no outer handle: the soul must open from within. World tours and engravings made it the most famous devotional image of its age.

title: King Cophetua and the Beggar Maid

image.alt: Edward Burne-Jones's King Cophetua and the Beggar Maid
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Burne-Jones's armored king sits at the feet of the beggar maid he will marry, in a jewel-toned vertical dream of brass and dusk. Its medieval romance and languid idealism crown late Pre-Raphaelite art.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A lone magpie perches on a wattle gate in fresh snow that casts blue shadows. Monet's luminous winter scene, rejected by the Salon, is now one of the most loved snowscapes in art.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Three bare-backed workers plane a Parisian parquet floor in raking window light. Rejected by the Salon as 'vulgar,' Caillebotte's urban laborers became an Impressionist landmark of modern subject matter.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A glazed-eyed woman sits before her absinthe glass beside a rumpled companion in a cafe, cropped off-center as if glimpsed in passing. Degas's clinical slice of modern life scandalized Victorian London.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Monet set his easel inside Paris's busiest rail terminus to paint locomotives dissolving into steam beneath the glass shed. The series made industrial modernity a fit subject for Impressionism.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Two fashionable women drift in a rowboat on the Bois de Boulogne lake, rendered in Morisot's flickering zigzag strokes. A quintessential Impressionist study of leisure, light, and water.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Renoir began this crowded Parisian street of bobbing umbrellas in feathery Impressionist style and finished it years later in crisp, Ingres-inspired drawing; the change of manner is visible across one canvas.

image.alt: Henri Rousseau's Tiger in a Tropical Storm (Surprised!)
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Rousseau's first jungle picture: a wide-eyed tiger crouches through wind-whipped, rain-slashed foliage. Mocked at the Salon des Independants, its stylized vegetation became his signature world.

image.alt: Henri de Toulouse-Lautrec's Moulin Rouge: La Goulue
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Lautrec's first poster: the dancer La Goulue kicks through the cancan between a silhouetted crowd and her lanky partner Valentin. Its flat shapes and bold cropping made the street poster modern art.

image.alt: Paul Gauguin's When Will You Marry?
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Two Tahitian women crouch among flat planes of green, yellow, and orange, one wearing a white marriage flower. In 2015 it made headlines as one of the most expensive paintings ever sold.

image.alt: Vincent van Gogh's Starry Night Over the Rhone
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Van Gogh painted Arles's gaslit quay at night from the riverbank itself, the Great Bear overhead and lamplight streaking gold across the water. The calmer nocturne that preceded The Starry Night.

image.alt: Vincent van Gogh's The Night Cafe
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Van Gogh's all-night Arles cafe glows in clashing blood-red and green under sulfurous lamps. He said he tried to show a place where one could 'ruin oneself, go mad, or commit a crime.'

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Cezanne's largest canvas locks nude bathers and arching trees into one architectural triangle. Left unresolved after seven years of work, its constructed forms opened the road to Matisse and Cubism.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A nude reclines on a velvet sofa improbably set in moonlit jungle thick with lions, lotuses, and a snake charmer's music. Rousseau's last great painting crowned his self-taught visionary style.

image.alt: Arnold Bocklin's Isle of the Dead
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A shrouded figure is rowed toward a cypress-filled rock island under a darkening sky. Bocklin's funereal vision, painted in five versions, hung in countless homes and obsessed Freud, Dali, and Rachmaninoff.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Watts's blindfolded Hope sits atop the globe, straining to hear the one unbroken string of her lyre. The Symbolist allegory inspired sermons and, through one of them, Barack Obama's 'Audacity of Hope.'

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Klimt sets a grinning skeleton in patterned robes against a tower of sleeping humanity wrapped in flowered quilts. The allegory won first prize at the 1911 International Art Exhibition in Rome.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Redon's gentle one-eyed Polyphemus peers shyly over a flowering hillside at the sleeping nymph Galatea. Painted in his late color period, it turns a monster myth into a tender Symbolist dreamscape.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Munch's dying sister Sophie rests against a pillow as her grieving aunt bows beside her, the paint scraped and scarred. He called it his breakthrough, the seed of his lifelong art of memory and loss.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Marc's young blue horse bows its head against rolling rainbow hills. For Der Blaue Reiter, blue stood for the spiritual; the painting remains German Expressionism's gentlest icon.

image.alt: Franz Marc's Fate of the Animals
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Marc's forest splinters into red shards as deer and horses twist beneath falling trees; he inscribed it 'all being is flaming agony.' Painted on the eve of World War I, in which he died at Verdun.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Schiele tilts his cropped head against sprigs of orange lantern fruit, his gaze at once defiant and wary. Its taut line and jagged self-scrutiny distill Viennese Expressionist portraiture.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Intruders torture a family in a cramped attic in Beckmann's nightmarish allegory of postwar Germany. Its compressed space and cold brutality mark Expressionism's turn toward the New Objectivity.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Braque shatters a violin and candlestick into shifting brown-gray facets seen from every angle at once. A textbook image of Analytic Cubism, painted in close daily dialogue with Picasso.

image.alt: Marcel Duchamp's Nude Descending a Staircase, No. 2
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Duchamp fractures a figure into staccato machine-like planes caught mid-descent. Rejected by the Cubists and mocked at the 1913 Armory Show as 'an explosion in a shingle factory,' it made him famous.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Gris renders his Bateau-Lavoir neighbor in crystalline blue facets, palette in hand: homage from Cubism's so-called third musketeer. Its ordered geometry announces his cooler, more systematic Cubism.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Leger assembles billboards, scaffolding, stenciled letters, and robotic figures into a jazzy urban machine. His tubular Cubism celebrates the modern metropolis instead of dissecting still lifes.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Picasso's masked Pierrot, Harlequin, and monk play music in flat interlocking planes like cut paper. The jaunty trio is the grand summation of Synthetic Cubism.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Balla's electric streetlamp radiates chevrons of colored light that outshine the crescent moon beside it. A Futurist manifesto in paint: modern technology triumphant over romantic nature.

title: The Funeral of the Anarchist Galli

image.alt: Carlo Carra's The Funeral of the Anarchist Galli
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Carra recalls a riot he witnessed at an anarchist's funeral as a whirl of black figures, lances, and red banners under slashing light. Futurist dynamism applied to political violence.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: A gaunt blind musician folds around his guitar in near-monochrome blue. The masterpiece of Picasso's Blue Period, painted in poverty in Barcelona, with a ghostly earlier figure visible beneath the paint.

image.alt: Hilma af Klint's The Ten Largest, No. 7, Adulthood
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: One of af Klint's ten monumental canvases charting the stages of life in swirling botanical forms and looping script, painted years before Kandinsky's abstraction and kept hidden for decades at her wish.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Modigliani's amber nude stretches across crimson velvet, her almond face serene. The 1917 nude series got his only solo show closed by Paris police; this canvas later sold for over $170 million.

title: I Saw the Figure 5 in Gold

image.alt: Charles Demuth's I Saw the Figure 5 in Gold
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Demuth's poster-portrait of poet William Carlos Williams recreates his poem: a gold 5 echoing through red fire-truck planes and city lights. A Precisionist landmark that anticipated Pop.

title: Cow's Skull: Red, White, and Blue

image.alt: Georgia O'Keeffe's Cow's Skull: Red, White, and Blue
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: O'Keeffe centers a bleached desert skull against a red, white, and blue band, half icon, half flag. Her wry answer to calls for the Great American Painting fused Southwest bones with national myth.

image.alt: Diego Rivera's Man at the Crossroads
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Rivera's Rockefeller Center mural was chiseled off the wall in 1934 after he refused to remove Lenin; he repainted it in Mexico City as Man, Controller of the Universe. The defining scandal of Depression-era mural art.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Mondrian's last completed painting pulses with small blocks of yellow, red, and blue along gridded avenues: Manhattan's lights set to boogie-woogie rhythm. His austere geometry learns to dance.

image.alt: Giorgio de Chirico's The Song of Love
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: A classical head, a surgeon's rubber glove, and a green ball meet on a melancholy arcaded square. De Chirico's Metaphysical juxtapositions moved Magritte to tears and seeded Surrealism.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Miro fills a blue-walled room with cavorting biomorphic creatures, music, and a ladder of escape, painted, he said, from hunger-induced hallucinations. A founding image of Surrealist automatism.

image.alt: Leonora Carrington's Self-Portrait (Inn of the Dawn Horse)
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Carrington perches wild-haired in a chair as a hyena mirrors her stance and a rocking horse gallops free outside the window. Her best-known canvas announces Surrealism's untamed feminine mythology.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Kahlo splits her torso open to reveal a crumbling Ionic column in place of her spine, her flesh studded with nails. Painted after spinal surgery, it is her starkest image of pain endured.

title: Christ of Saint John of the Cross

image.alt: Salvador Dali's Christ of Saint John of the Cross
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Dali suspends Christ on a cross seen steeply from above, floating over the bay of Port Lligat without nails or blood. Based on a Spanish mystic's drawing, it is his most popular religious work.

image.alt: Rene Magritte's The Son of Man
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Magritte's bowler-hatted self-portrait, its face hidden behind a hovering green apple, plays on the visible that hides the visible. It became the universal shorthand for Surrealism.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: De Kooning's slashing, wide-eyed woman fuses figure and ground in aggressive strokes, one of six Women from 1951-1953. Quietly traded out of Tehran in 1994, it later sold for $137.5 million.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Newman's eighteen-foot field of cadmium red, divided by five thin vertical 'zips,' was meant to be seen from up close, engulfing the viewer. Its Latin title proposes man, heroic and sublime.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Frankenthaler stained thinned paint directly into raw canvas, letting washes of blue, pink, and green bloom like watercolor. Painted at twenty-three, it launched Color Field painting.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Three soft-edged rectangles of saturated orange, red, and yellow hover on a vermilion field. In 2012 it set the auction record for postwar art, emblematic of Rothko's color-field sublimity.

title: Elegy to the Spanish Republic No. 110

image.alt: Robert Motherwell's Elegy to the Spanish Republic No. 110
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Black ovals and slab verticals march across a white field in Motherwell's recurring funeral chant for the Spanish Civil War. He painted more than one hundred Elegies; No. 110 hangs in the Guggenheim.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Johns rebuilt the American flag in encaustic over collaged newsprint, making a painting that is also the thing it depicts. Its deadpan object-ness broke with Abstract Expressionism and fed Pop.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Lifted from a romance comic panel, a tearful heroine in stylized waves declares she would 'rather sink than call Brad for help.' Benday dots and melodrama distilled into a Pop Art icon.

title: Just What Is It That Makes Today's Homes So Different, So Appealing?

image.alt: Richard Hamilton's Just What Is It That Makes Today's Homes So Different, So Appealing?
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Hamilton furnishes a modern living room from magazine ads: a bodybuilder with a giant POP lollipop, a pin-up, a Ford logo, a tin of ham. Made for the 1956 show This Is Tomorrow, it is Pop Art's founding collage.

title: Portrait of an Artist (Pool with Two Figures)

image.alt: David Hockney's Portrait of an Artist (Pool with Two Figures)
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: A clothed man gazes down at a swimmer gliding underwater in a sunlit pool against Provencal hills. In 2018 it briefly became the most expensive work by a living artist ever auctioned.

title: Study after Velazquez's Portrait of Pope Innocent X

image.alt: Francis Bacon's Study after Velazquez's Portrait of Pope Innocent X
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Bacon traps Velazquez's pope in a cage of vertical streaks, mouth torn open in a silent scream. The most famous of his roughly forty-five 'screaming popes.'

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: A grinning blue-black skull-head crackles with graffiti energy over a subway-blue field. Painted at twenty-one, it sold for $110.5 million in 2017, then the auction record for any American artist.

image.alt: Chris Ofili's No Woman, No Cry
image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Ofili's weeping woman holds a tiny photograph of murdered teenager Stephen Lawrence in every tear, the canvas propped on varnished elephant dung. The Turner Prize winner's most moving meditation on grief.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Freud's monumental nude of Sue Tilley asleep on a battered sofa renders flesh in thick, unidealized impasto. In 2008 it set the auction record for a work by a living artist.

image.license: Fair use in source context; replace with licensed image before production if needed

details.gloss: Banksy's stencil of a girl reaching for a heart-shaped balloon first appeared on a London bridge. In 2018 a framed version shredded itself at Sotheby's the moment it sold, becoming 'Love Is in the Bin.'

image.alt: Katsushika Hokusai's The Great Wave off Kanagawa
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Hokusai's woodblock print sends a clawed wave towering over fishing boats, Mount Fuji tiny in the trough. The most reproduced image in Japanese art, it shaped Impressionism and Japonisme in the West.

image.alt: Katsushika Hokusai's Fine Wind, Clear Morning (Red Fuji)
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Mount Fuji glows red against a blue sky combed with cloud, snow receding from its summit at dawn. From the same Thirty-six Views series as the Great Wave, it is Japan's most celebrated mountain image.

title: Sudden Shower over Shin-Ohashi Bridge and Atake

image.alt: Utagawa Hiroshige's Sudden Shower over Shin-Ohashi Bridge and Atake
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Pedestrians dash across a wooden bridge under slanting lines of sudden rain, from One Hundred Famous Views of Edo. Van Gogh copied it in oils, making it a touchstone of Japonisme.

title: Three Beauties of the Present Day

image.alt: Kitagawa Utamaro's Three Beauties of the Present Day
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Utamaro groups three celebrated Edo beauties in a triangular bust composition, their faces near-identical yet subtly distinct. A masterpiece of bijin-ga, the ukiyo-e genre of beautiful women.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Tohaku's pair of six-panel screens conjures pines emerging from mist with bold wet ink and vast emptiness. A pinnacle of Japanese ink painting in the Zen-inflected spirit of suggestion over statement.

image.alt: Tawaraya Sotatsu's Wind God and Thunder God
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Sotatsu flings the green thunder god and white wind god across gold-leaf screens, leaving the center empty. The defining image of the decorative Rinpa school, later repainted by Korin in homage.

title: Along the River During the Qingming Festival

image.alt: Zhang Zeduan's Along the River During the Qingming Festival
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Zhang Zeduan's five-meter handscroll teems with boats, bridges, markets, and hundreds of figures along the Bian River at the Song capital. Often called China's Mona Lisa, it defined the panoramic city scroll.

title: A Thousand Li of Rivers and Mountains

image.alt: Wang Ximeng's A Thousand Li of Rivers and Mountains
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Wang Ximeng painted this eleven-meter blue-green landscape scroll at eighteen for Emperor Huizong and died soon after. Its malachite-and-azurite peaks are a summit of Song dynasty painting.

image.alt: Fan Kuan's Travelers Among Mountains and Streams
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: A monumental cliff hangs over tiny travelers and their mules in Fan Kuan's Northern Song masterwork. Its towering composition fixed the ideal of Chinese monumental landscape painting for centuries.

image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Five peaks flank a red sun and white moon over rolling waves in this Joseon court screen, placed behind the king's throne as a cosmic emblem of royal authority. It appears on South Korea's 10,000-won note.

image.alt: Sultan Muhammad's The Court of Gayumars
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Sultan Muhammad's miniature for Shah Tahmasp's Shahnameh sets the first king's mountain court in a swirling, jewel-colored paradise of rocks and clouds. Widely held to be the finest Persian painting ever made.

title: Jahangir Preferring a Sufi Shaikh to Kings

image.alt: Bichitr's Jahangir Preferring a Sufi Shaikh to Kings
image.license: Public domain or freely licensed via Wikimedia Commons

details.gloss: Bichitr's Mughal miniature seats Emperor Jahangir on an hourglass throne, handing a book to a Sufi saint while kings wait below, James I of England among them. Allegory as imperial propaganda.
